title: Quick check: What an agent is

questions.prompt: What's the defining property that separates an agent from a chatbot or pipeline?

choices.text: Agents decide their own next step based on intermediate results, within boundaries you set. Chatbots react to user messages one at a time; pipelines follow a fixed developer-defined sequence. The LLM choosing what to do next is the agent property.

questions.explanation: The agent property is autonomous next-step decision-making. Tools are common but not unique to agents (chatbots can have tools). The 'agent' word is overloaded — keep the definition tight: agent = the LLM picks the path. If the path is fixed, it's a pipeline, no matter what you call it.

questions.prompt: Your customer support team wants to triage emails: classify intent, extract sender info, route to the right queue, log to CRM. Every email goes through these same 4 steps. Agent or pipeline?

choices.text: Agent — anything with tools is an agent.

Pipeline. There's no decision-making — every email takes the same path. Pipelines are simpler, cheaper, more reliable, and faster than agents for fixed sequences. Use agents when the path itself depends on intermediate results.

questions.explanation: Fixed sequence with no branching = pipeline. The 'agent' framing buys nothing here — same code, more overhead. Save agents for cases where the LLM genuinely has to decide what to do next based on results from earlier steps.
